food_subgroup: Steamed rice flour string noodle nests

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Idiyappam. Nearest parent is a steamed rice flour noodle product. A new concept is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice noodle products — steamed rice flour noodle nests (closest parent)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of idiyappam is 3 nests (approximately 60g each, 180g total) — each nest is a flat circular arrangement of delicate extruded rice flour vermicelli steamed until tender, typically served with coconut milk and curry.

nutrient_source.title: Idiyappam — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, derived from rice flour composition data; adapted from puttu/idiyappam preparation
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN does not have a standalone idiyappam entry. Values are derived from ICMR-NIN rice flour composition data and standard idiyappam recipe (rice flour ~45%, water ~52%, salt, small amount of oil). Cross-referenced with comparable vermicelli rice noodle entries.

gi_source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from steamed rice vermicelli; GI is high due to the refined white rice flour with minimal fibre and protein to slow digestion.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium approximately 216mg per 3-nest serving (180g) — low; idiyappam is one of the lowest-sodium cooked grain products.
hypertension.guidance: Excellent choice for hypertensive patients; virtually sodium-free in the idiyappam itself. Sodium intake is primarily determined by the accompaniments (curry, coconut milk).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (~74) and high carbohydrate content from refined white rice flour; glycaemic load per 3-nest serving (180g) is approximately 49 — very high.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Diabetic patients should limit to 1–2 nests and pair with high-protein accompaniments (egg, chicken stew, lentils) to moderate the glycaemic response. Ragi idiyappam (finger millet) is a lower-GI alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Extremely low in fat and allergens; rice flour is non-inflammatory; the dish itself is very pure.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — gluten-free, vegan, and very low in fat. Pair with anti-inflammatory curries containing turmeric and ginger.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Idiyappam (also known as Putu Mayam in the Malay-Singapore context) is a delicate South Indian and Sri Lankan dish of steamed rice flour noodles. A soft dough of rice flour and water is pressed through a mould with fine perforations to create thin vermicelli-like strands, which are arranged into flat nests and steamed until tender. Each nest is white, slightly translucent, and has a soft, silky texture. It is virtually fat-free and naturally gluten-free. In Singapore, putu mayam is sold by street vendors with freshly grated coconut and palm sugar (gula melaka), and also served at South Indian restaurants with coconut milk and curry.

confidence_reason: No standalone idiyappam entry in ICMR-NIN. Values derived from ICMR-NIN rice flour data and standard recipe proportions. Estimates are consistent with similar steamed rice flour products.
notes: Vegan and gluten-free. Per_100g values are for plain idiyappam only (no coconut milk or sugar). Accompaniments (sweetened coconut milk + palm sugar from street vendor putu mayam) would add approximately 60–80 kcal and 15–18g sugar per serving. GI is notably high due to refined rice flour — ragi (finger millet) idiyappam is a better choice for diabetic patients.
